title: ASEAN’s critical minerals and industrial-upgrade dilemma
author: IISS
text:
Wakana Asano @Wakana_AsanoResearch Associate, API Takano FellowshipAbout Wakana

Online Analysis5th June 2026

ASEAN’s critical minerals and industrial-upgrade dilemmaASEAN intends to undergo a strategic pivot to become a high-value manufacturing hub for critical minerals. Financial constraints and external dependencies, however, may make this challenging.

Indonesia accounts for over 60% of the global nickel supply. The Philippines is the world’s largest nickel-ore exporter and possesses an estimated US$170 billion in nickel deposits. Combined with broader reserves in tin, copper, bauxite and rare earth elements (REEs), Southeast Asian states, many of which are Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) member states, have become more important than ever to global supply chains for high-tech industries. As ASEAN states seek to leverage their globally comparative advantages in critical minerals, the region must navigate a complex environment shaped by geo-economic competition, industrial policy and supply-chain restructuring while simultaneously attracting investment. Although individual states compete economically and are making efforts to strengthen their domestic industries, ASEAN also faces growing pressure to coordinate regional supply chains, facilitate economic integration and reduce vulnerability to external economic and geopolitical shocks.
For ASEAN, critical minerals are not only export commodities, but also a foundation for industrial upgrading and deeper integration into emerging technology-value chains. Yet the region’s ambitions for a mineral-based economic growth may also deepen external dependence on concentrated refining and processing ecosystems. This, combined with intra-ASEAN economic competition, creates a broader dilemma for ASEAN’s critical-mineral strategy amid intensifying global geo-economic turmoil.
Complex yet promising
As one of the world’s top suppliers of major minerals and metal products, ASEAN views critical minerals as a key opportunity for industrial transformation and economic growth. The ASEAN Minerals Cooperation Action Plan 2026-2030, released in September 2025, and the ASEAN Minerals Development Vision reflect growing interest among ASEAN member states in strengthening regional integration on critical-mineral supply chains and downstream industries. Yet the bloc is likely to face challenges in collectively implementing these mineral-related strategies, given the competition between member states in attracting foreign direct investment, the differences in their industrial levels, the geopolitical nature of critical minerals and the associated financing requirements.
Critical minerals also present ASEAN with an opportunity to undergo structural industrial upgrades. ASEAN is seeking to pivot from primarily exporting raw materials to developing domestic refining, processing and manufacturing capacities. The objective is to transition from low-value raw-mineral exports toward higher-value downstream processing and manufacturing within the global critical-minerals industry, which generally has higher economic outcomes for producing countries. Complementing this strategy, ASEAN has identified several mineral-dependent, high-growth sectors as important to its economic agenda. These include electric vehicles, lithium-ion batteries, solar photovoltaics and semiconductors. Critical minerals are key to energy diversification as a wide range of renewable-energy technologies require them, from copper for energy-transmission grids to nickels for battery storage. As many countries worldwide increase their renewable-energy usage and diversify their energy sources to in order to ensure their energy security, combined with the increasing spread of artificial intelligence (AI) and rearmament, global demand for critical minerals is likely to further increase.
For ASEAN member states, critical minerals are therefore strategic assets for both their own economic growth and deeper integration into global markets. Even with economic and geopolitical differences within the bloc, such greater regional cooperation would still provide significant strategic and economic advantages in the wider global market – helping to cushion external geopolitical and economic shocks and strengthening investor confidence – that are difficult to achieve individually.
Industrial upgrading and increasing geo-economic risks
In the context of critical raw materials, ASEAN today largely functions as an upstream exporter of critical raw materials, but as mentioned, the bloc has increasingly aimed to transition toward higher-value downstream industries. However, this ambition creates a structural vulnerability. Moving up the value chain requires greater access to refined materials, processing technology and midstream industrial capacity, which are all still heavily dominated by China. China accounts for more than 90% of global REEs-refining capacity, and 60–70% for lithium and cobalt. As ASEAN deepens its industrial upgrading and downstream-manufacturing ambitions, it may simultaneously become more dependent on China’s near-absolute control over refining capacities, especially for nickel and REEs. In this sense, ASEAN seeks to reduce its reliance on commodity exports and build greater value within emerging-technology industries, yet the current structure of global critical-mineral supply chains may constrain its ability to do so independently.
Vulnerabilities in the supply chain create choke points where a single country or region can disrupt the global supply. Such choke points can emerge at multiple stages of the minerals supply chain, from upstream mining and extraction, midstream processing, to downstream consumption. Within ASEAN, high geographical concentration and dependence, such as nickel, cobalt and copper production being concentrated in Indonesia and the Philippines, leads to high exposure to market shocks and geopolitical disruptions. As ASEAN seeks to coordinate regional critical-mineral strategies and industries, disruptions affecting these minerals in its member states would create broader supply-chain, market and industrial consequences across the bloc.
Financing constraints
Developing new mines and processing facilities involves long lead times, making it difficult to respond quickly to sudden surges in demand or cuts in supply. Like many emerging economies, ASEAN is still improving its infrastructure and investment environment. Building end-to-end supply chains requires substantial long-term capital investment.
In response, external financing initiatives and strategic industrial partnerships have become more important. In May 2026, the Asian Development Bank launched new financing tools to help Asia-Pacific economies reduce supply-chain dependence and concentration. Similarly, Japan has expanded strategic cooperation with and financing for Southeast Asia through initiatives such as the Asia Zero Emission Community and the Partnership on Wide Energy and Resources Resilience in April 2026, later joined by the World Bank in June 2026. While governments and multilateral development banks have promoted public–private partnerships to address infrastructure and investment gaps in the region, the scale and complexity of these requirements create high barriers to entry. This constrains the pace at which mineral-supply chains and markets integrate both regionally and globally.
At the same time, several key midstream processing capacities are heavily dependent on Chinese investment. In Indonesia, Chinese firms control around 75% of the country’s nickel-refining capacity, including via major investments in industrial complexes. While the United States has just signed deals to increase investment in critical minerals in several ASEAN states, these are still in their early stages. It will take time to mobilise capital, build infrastructure and supply chains and establish an equivalent regional influence to that of China. This creates a structural vulnerability for Indonesia and, more broadly, for ASEAN.
Regional economic growth and competition
Following the launch of Pax Silica in December 2025 and the US Critical Minerals Ministerial in February 2026, states around the world have become more explicitly involved in American tech and financing alliances based around critical minerals. Several Asian middle powers, such as Australia, India, Japan, Singapore and South Korea are core participants in these new American-led initiatives. Combined with recent US bilateral critical-mineral deals with Indonesia, Malaysia and Thailand, ASEAN will likely see new economic opportunities for investment, industrial upgrading and integration into more diversified supply chains. ASEAN can support its ambitions for regional economic growth by continuing to expand partnerships with these technologically advanced economies, particularly in high-tech sectors. Moreover, compared with other resource-rich regions like Africa and Latin America, for Asian partners ASEAN offers the advantages of geographic proximity to Asia’s manufacturing supply chain and growing economies.
On the other hand, these developments may also intensify geo-economic fragmentation within ASEAN. While some member states may seek closer alignment with these US-led supply-chain frameworks, others may continue to prioritise economic or ideological ties with China. Differences in national industrialisation levels, political orientation and strategic priorities generate divergent approaches to critical-mineral governance and supply-chain alignment within ASEAN. As critical minerals become increasingly crucial to national security globally, ASEAN may face growing challenges in maintaining regional cohesion while balancing competing economic and strategic systems.
ASEAN’s minerals industry amid global tensions
ASEAN’s abundant critical raw-material resources provide it with a significant opportunity to strengthen its position in emerging energy-, advanced-manufacturing and strategic-supply chains. Yet mineral wealth alone does not guarantee long-term industrial transformation. As global demand for AI, data centres and renewable energy accelerates and geo-economic competition intensifies, ASEAN will face the dual challenge of determining whether the region can secure financial and supply-chain capital and capture greater value beyond upstream extraction.
The structure of contemporary critical-mineral-supply chains presents both opportunities and constraints. External financing and regional-integration initiatives may support ASEAN’s industrial transformation, but they may also deepen dependence on externally dominated processing ecosystems and vulnerability to geopolitical competitions. Whether ASEAN’s mineral wealth becomes a foundation for long-term industrial transformation or a new form of dependence may ultimately depend on who finances, processes and integrates the region into the next generation of strategic supply chains.  

AuthorWakana Asano Research Associate, API Takano Fellowship
